sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE]
Moishe.
A nickname?
No, I used Moishe, Moses, Misha.
And Mark.
I used Moishe, Misha, Moses, and Mark.
No, I need a long sentence basically.
I sold my furniture to my son.
And I moved in with my son for three days.
And then I moved here.
Yeah.
Well, I was just in between moving.
Everything was packed up.
And the mover came, and got the stuff, and then I-- then I moved here.
Yeah, my niece's husband drove me down here.
He volunteered.
Yeah.
Mark Zilist.
Moishe Zilist.
Misha.
October, 14, 1929.
I was born in a small town in Ukraine called Romanov.
Romanov, which is near Zhytomyr.
Yes.
Ukraine.
It was mainly a farmer work.
And it was a small town.
It was just a few stores.
My father was running a grocery store that was belonged to the government.
And he was the manager.
Oh, roughly 5,000, 6,000.
Of Romanov.
Of--
Yes.
Some Jews were farmers, yes.
And businesspeople.
It was under government supervision.
It was a good relationship, because, see, we were under the communist regime.
And nobody was allowed to discriminate against anybody.
Yes, I went to a public school, Russian school, that I finished sixth grade.
There was no problem, no problem.
Teachers-- the schoolmates-- there was no problem-- no anti-Semitism or no discrimination.
Not for my age.
My parents were probably--
--years back.
I've heard some grownups talking about it from years back but not during the communist regime.
They said that they had-- they were mistreated and looked down up on.
But that was before the communist regime.
Yes.
In my house, we spoke Yiddish and Russian.
Both.
My family father and mother were religious, but not orthodox religious.
By that-- is that-- see, under the communist regime, we were not allowed to practice religion.
So the Jewish people would get together in each other's house, and have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prayers, and had minyans, and they had prayers and so forth.
And Friday night, they'd get together for Shabbos.
But it was all illegal and all undercover.
But at the same token, nobody was bothered about that.
Say that again.
What is rituals?
Yes.
Yes.
Kosher.
It was a kosher home.
Yeah.
A [NON-ENGLISH], yes.
And my mother did all the cooking, all the baking.
And it was-- it was kosher.
Shabbos was observed only in the house.
Meaning that Friday night we had Shabbos dinner.
And my mother lit candles.
But as far as the outside is concerned, if my father had to work on Shabbos, he did it, only because the government told him to do that.
Yiddish paper and Russian paper.
Both.
No.
My mother was a housewife.
And only my father worked.
No.
I had an older sister, step-sister.
She was roughly about six or seven year-- seven years older than I was.
It was my father's second marriage.
His first wife died.
And he had the daughter by his first wife.
And then he married my mother.
My sister?
[NON-ENGLISH], Rochelle.
11 Years old.
11 Years old.
And your sister would have been.
That was in 1929-- almost 12 years.
I was 12 and 1/2 years old.
So my sister was 18, 19.
No, she was in the last year of school.
Yes.
None at all.
No, no.
I did know when the war broke out in 1939, I heard about that.
We heard that the war broke out in Poland and that the Germans were killing Jews.
And I'm sure that my parents were more knowledgeable of them than I was, because I was just a youngster.
But I remember some of it vaguely.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A year or two before the war broke out, that's the first time that we had a radio in the house.
And the radio consisted of the speaker only.
And whatever the government broadcasted on the radio, that's what we heard.
We couldn't tune in to no stations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a well for water with hothouses outside for toilets.
It was a very [NON-ENGLISH] old-fashioned town.
By the rabbi, yeah, by a rabbi a religious person that would come to the house and teach me the Torah.
But half of the time when he came, I-- I wasn't home.
I didn't want to learn.
[LAUGHS]
I liked school.
And I liked to play outside.
And I had a rabbit as my pet.
I raised rabbits.
And when my mother would buy-- see, my mother used to go to the-- to the open-air market and by live chickens.
And we put them in cages.
And I used to like to feed the chickens.
We-- we fed them to fatten them up and then take them, and slaughter them, and have them for dinner.
The rabbi would come to the house two or three times a week.
Out of the two or three times, I was with him once or twice a week.
I remember that vaguely.
No.
No.
No.
We were not allowed to do that.
In 1941-- either June or July?
Probably July.
Right after they occupied the town, shortly after, one morning they walk-- they came into the house.
They knocked on the door.
They woke us up.
They told us to get dressed and go outside.
They round up all the Jews from town.
And they put us in a large building like a-- where soldiers are-- like a--
Armory, exactly.
And after they round up all the Jews, they started to sort out professionals.
By that-- what I mean by that is tailors, shoemakers, and young children up to a certain age.
And they let us go.
And the rest of them, they killed all in one day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when they let me go, I was left by myself.
[DRINKS] They were led to the woods.
And there was a grave dug-- massive graves dug.
And they were killed.
And they were--
I heard the shots.
And they never came back.
And we were told that's what happened to them.
The-- after I was let go, I stayed with one of my neighbors.
He was a tailor.
So they let him go and his whole family.
And that's what they talked about.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never had a chance to go to the grave.
But this is what we were told.
And we heard shots.
My family and all the rest of the Jewish people in my town.
Yes.
Yes.
That was August 25, 1941.
I stayed with my neighbor until they decided to round up all the professionals and children and kill them, too.
So when they came in to-- to the man that I stayed with, and they told them to go outside, I hid in back of a door.
And they didn't see me.
So after they left, I went-- I crawled on top of the attic in the house.
And I stayed there for a day and a half.
I was petrified.
I was scared to go out.
I was scared to move.
I was scared to do anything.
And then I had to go outside.
When I go out, I was hiding in hot houses and abandoned houses.
During that time, the Ukrainian police that was working with the Germans and the Nazis, they were searching the town for-- for Jews that was left over.
And two times, they caught me.
And they put me in jail.
And during the day, they would round up women and children and put everybody in one cell. 5 o'clock in the evening, they would let us out to the woods.
And they had a grave dug.
And they would kill everybody.
And I managed to run away both times.
I was-- easing off-- we were led in the woods.
And there was guards on the side and in the back.
But I was easing off to the side, and easing off to the side, and just took off and ran.
And either they saw it and didn't see it.
Or, they didn't see me run away.
Right.
The last time that I ran away, I decided not to go back in town, because it's-- I would have been killed too.
So I decided to walk-- I was scared of the front line.
So I decided to walk to Poland.
So I sat down, and I made up of facticious ID, ID with a Ukrainian or Russian name.
And I put it in my pocket.
And I just walked so many miles every day until it got dark.
And then I would come to a village and beg for bread, beg for food, beg for a place to stay.
And if I didn't have a place to stay, I would sleep outside.
It led on.
It was like in the fall.
So during the winter, I remember sleeping in the snow one night.
And I never caught a cold.
And this is how I kept on going unbeknown to me where I was going to wind up or what I was going to do.
No.
[DRINKS]
No.
Nobody ever asked me for ID.
I passed by German soldiers on the road-- Ukrainian police, people, nobody ever stopped me for anything.
I was dirty with torn up clothes.
And I just looked like a homeless person.
And they never paid attention to me.
Then I was a kid, too.
So they didn't-- they didn't think that I was Jewish.
That's the main reason why I was never stopped
The only time that I was asked if I was Jewish is when I-- I reached the destination.
I went into this man's house.
And he had an office.
He was in the lumber business.
But on this trip, we never asked if you are Jew?
During the trip, no.
Only one time.
Maybe 10, or maybe 20.
No.
I never knew any name of the places that I was in.
Because I never was interested in the names.
I was interested in two things, to survive, find a place to work, and survive the-- the war.
One night, one-- one night, this man took me in to his house.
And he knew that I was Jewish without even me telling him.
And he kept me for two days.
And the night before I-- I left, he told me to leave early in the morning.
He sat down.
And he talked to me for about two or three hours.
He was a very religious man, a young man, but a very religious and very much read up in the Bible.
And he had quotations from the Bible hanging on the walls.
That and by talking to him.
He quoted quotations from the Bible.
Gentile Bible.
I don't know what religion he was.
But any rate, he told me a lot what's going to happen to me in the future.
And believe it or not, most of-- 90% of it came true.
He told me that I'm going to survive.
[DRINKS] He told me that I was going to be successful.
Above all, he told me not to take revenge.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he emphasized that very strongly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after all these years, I remember that.
[DRINKS] And he told me that after I leave him, not to worry, that soon I will find a place to stay.
And I did.
And I got to this place-- to this man that had a-- he was in the lumber business.
And I asked him for a job.
And he gave me a job as a house boy.
And I stayed with him maybe a few months.
During the time that I stayed with him, he questioned me periodically if I was Jewish.
And I told him no, no, and no.
Until one day, I took the horses into the field-- into the pasture.
And it was near the woods.
And there was some other kids with horses, and cows, and the pasture.
And some of the kids were playing with fire.
And they set fire in the woods.
So I took the horses.
And I came home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I told him what happened.
And he didn't say anything.
A week later, I was in the barn.
And he came in.
And he says, there's German soldiers in my office.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [DRINKS]
They want to talk to you.
Are you Jewish or not?
I said, yes, I am.
He says, do you want to face them?
Or do you want to run away?
I said, I'm going to face them.
What made  you--
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The older I get, the more I believe that there was some spirit guiding me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What made me say that and what made me do that, I don't know.
But this is what I did.
So he took me into the office.
I sat down by the table.
And the Germans were across the table right in front of me.
And the first thing they asked me if I smoke.
And I told them no .
And the next thing they asked me whether I know anything-- I know who set the fire.
What it turned out to be is they came to investigate who set up the fire in the woods.
And I told them I don't know who did it.
But there was some more kids in there.
And one of them set the fire.
And I don't know who.
But I don't smoke.
And they turned me.
They told me I can go.
No.
[LAUGHS] [DRINKS] It felt good in a way and bad in another way that I told the man-- I gave myself away.
I told my man-- the man who I was.
Luckily, he took me in the house.
And  he sat down he and told-- after everybody left.
And he told me.
He says, look, he says, I had suspicion all along that you were Jewish.
Now, that I know that you're Jewish, he said, I cannot keep you here.
And the penalties was that if the Germans found a Jewish person in the house, they killed the whole family and burned the house down.
He knew that.
And he told me that.
And I knew that.
He says, but don't worry about it.
He says, I'm not going to turn you in.
I'm going to find you a farmer that is looking for a house boy.
And I'll get you a job with him.
You'll live with him.
And I'll never tell nobody nothing about you.
And that's what happened.
He found me a place where the farmer needed a house boy.
And I lived with him and worked with him-- got beating-- took beatings every day.
He was a young farmer.
He was a drinker.
He was a womanizer.
And whether he knew who I was or he didn't he didn't know I was, he took advantage of me.
He taught me how to plow in the field.
Then he would come out.
And he would look and say, that's not straight.
And he took the whip that I was whipping the horse.
And-- [DRINKS] and he would whip me across the hand.
I couldn't complain.
I couldn't say anything, because I wanted to survive.
Later on, as time passed, I was on this farm for at least two years or maybe more.
But later, on as time passed by, he was found dead on another farm in a-- in a barn.
He got drunk, or he got in a fight.
And somebody killed him.
So his wife was left with two children.
And I was the man of the house, and taking care of the farm, and everything that needs to be taken care of until the Germans started retreating.
And one day, we saw the Germans retreating.
And I was outside.
And I had the horse and the wagon in back of the house.
So when I saw that, I jumped in a wagon.
And I took off running with the horse and wagon into the field.
His wife and the two children were scared.
And the Germans were shooting at me.
And they never hit the horse, the wagon, or me.
His wife got scared.
And they had a hole dug in the middle of the room where they used to keep food in the summertime, because it is cold underneath the ground.
Pardon me?
An Earth floor, yes.
Yes.
So she took the children and should crawled into that hole trying to hide from the Germans.
After I ran away, the Germans set the house on fire, And she got killed with the children-- suffocated.
Yes.
No.
[DRINKS]
I just worked for room and board.
Everybody slept in one room.
I slept on the side near-- there was a brick open where she used to go to cooking.
And there was a small-- small space between the wall and the oven.
And that's where I slept on a board-- on a wooden board.
I would say so, yeah, yes.
Valodka.
Valodka.
Did you have to change anything else about yourself?
No.
[DRINKS] The only other thing that I had to do was keep my hair close cut real close.
Because I have curly hair.
And gentile people in Europe don't have curly hair per se as a whole-- Ukrainians, Russians.
So my curly hair would give me away.
So I-- I cut my hair almost every week with the clippers.
I had one of the farmers do that for me before it grew up-- it grew out and extended where it started curling.
So it wasn't noticeable.
Where I got all that knowledge as a kid to do all these things, I had the slightest-- I don't have the slightest idea.
I don't know.
Like I say, something was guiding me and telling me what to do and how to do it.
And that's what I did until 1944 when the-- that night-- that day when the Germans was-- were retreating, I went in-- in a-- in a field.
And the lady that I stayed with, she had a sister that lived-- she had a shack in the field.
She lived with her husband and two children.
So I came to her after I ran away.
And  she told me that since everybody is dead and the houses burned down, I could stay with her.
So I stayed with her for a few days.
And then word came that the Russians are in the village, which means that the town near the village was liberated.
I got up one morning.
And I was scared to tell them where I was going.
And I was scared to tell him I was Jewish.
So I just got up one morning, and I left and didn't say nothing to anybody.
I left, I went on the main road.
Summer.
I got on the main road, and I was walking towards Ostrog.
O-S-T-R-O-G.
It was-- it was Poland.
And at times-- at time-- at times, it was Russian.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But at the time during the war, it belonged to Poland.
When I came to-- so when I was walking on the road, a wagon came.
A farmer had a load of hay stacked up real high.
And he was going towards-- towards that town-- towards Ostrog.
I jumped up.
I caught the back of the wagon.
And I pulled myself up on top of the hay.
And the man never knew that I was up there, because he was in the front of the hay controlling the horse and didn't see or didn't hear anything about it.
As soon as we got on the outskirts of town, I jumped off the wagon.
And I started walking into town.
And he never knew I was there.
